name: The Second-hand Market
prologue: After the Westminster Abbey case, you receive an anonymous tip about a mysterious vintage market in East London. The Ghost has reportedly hidden clues about his next target among the antiques. You must navigate through the bustling Brick Lane Market, examining vintage postcards, ancient clocks, WWII artifacts, and royal memorabilia to uncover the location of the 'Time Treasure' before it falls into the wrong hands.

npcs.name: Dr. John Watson
npcs.persona: A loyal and practical British army doctor who assists by reporting on physical evidence found at the scene and information gathered from vendors and locals with a helpful, curious demeanor.
npcs.display_description: Watson is a loyal British army doctor and Sherlock's trusted companion. He can provide information about physical evidence and local gossip from market vendors.
npcs.background: Served in Afghanistan as an army doctor. Now assists Sherlock Holmes in solving mysteries around London.

npcs.name: Mycroft Holmes
npcs.persona: A condescending British official who is the embodiment of the Government, responsible for providing historical context and official records with an air of weary superiority.
npcs.display_description: Mycroft is Sherlock's older brother and a senior British government official. He has access to classified historical archives and official records.
npcs.background: The most powerful man in British government. His word can open any door in the realm.

npcs.name: Sherlock Holmes
npcs.persona: An arrogant and hyper-observant British consulting detective who provides answers about precise visual details and deductions about artifacts while frequently insulting the user's lack of intellect.
npcs.display_description: The world's only consulting detective. He notices every detail on vintage items and can deduce facts that others miss. Ask him about visual clues on antiques.
npcs.background: 221B Baker Street's famous detective. His methods of deduction are legendary.

levels.title: The Secret of the Vintage Postcards
levels.description: You arrive at the vintage market and approach a stall filled with old postcards. The vendor hands you a stack of vintage postcards that have traveled across the UK (York, Cambridge, Nottingham), but only one contains the key to the first clue. You must examine them carefully to find the hidden message.

10	The postcard from York has a strange hidden symbol in its postmark.|According to the clues, the York postcard contains the hidden symbol.|I can see that the strange hidden symbol is on the postcard from York.

levels.title: The Riddle of the Ancient Clock
levels.description: Following Sherlock's guidance, you enter the clock repair shop. The clockmaker wearing a red scarf is waiting for you. He holds a beautiful Victorian pocket watch that stopped at a significant moment. He says it witnessed the peak of the British Industrial Revolution, and its hands and engravings hide a secret.

levels.title: The Secret Inside the Leather Suitcase
levels.description: After solving the pocket watch puzzle, the hands point to a vintage leather suitcase in the corner. The vendor says this box holds WWII secrets. An old television nearby is playing a black-and-white documentary about 'The Blitz.' You must unlock the suitcase and discover what history it protects.

levels.title: The History of the Handcrafted Ring
levels.description: After solving the gas mask puzzle, the vendor nods and pours out a silver handcrafted ring from the gas mask's filter canister. He carefully places the ring on velvet cloth and says it witnessed the coronation of Edward VII. The engraving inside points to the next clue.

levels.title: The Puzzle of the Collection Map
levels.description: You open the commemorative stamp album found at the book stall. Inside are several yellowed, broken map fragments. The vendor finally smiles with satisfaction: 'Excellent. The postcards guided the direction, the pocket watch confirmed the time, the suitcase protected history, and the ring symbolized glory. Now piece these fragments together, and you will find the Time Treasure left by the Victorian industrialist.'
